change_summary: Queue item 232: direct patches applied post-c9 — finding field rewritten to retract the 'empirically calibrated per-station' language in favor of 'observation-anchored rather than dome-derived,' and detail_evidence paragraph 3 reframed from 'by contrast' transition to concession-then-pivot on FSF = H(r)/r.
our_argument_summary: Std Model Explains: Chapman (1933) Sq current mechanism + Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble predict Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30–50° over ~180-min windows without any dome parameters; the dome admits this at Tier 1 of its Steelman Evidence Ladder; FSF = H(r)/r station-scaling is a genuine dome derivation but is not invoked for the 1991–2016 ensemble counted here, and where it is invoked (2026 forward projection) the absolute magnitude is imported from the 2017 BOU observation; WIN-010/025/068 triple-count one ionospheric Sq mechanism.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py file for WIN-068 in raw-text snapshot. raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21 defines the 2026 forward formula as 'delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF' with FSF = field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry. Nine per-station predictions listed (lines 26–34). WIN-068 itself is the 39-eclipse 1991–2016 retrospective Kim & Chang citation and has no associated computation in the dome monitor.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: WIN-068 has no monitor check at all (monitoring='none'). The 2026 forward projection in raw-text/03-predictions.txt uses station-by-station tabulated FSF values (0.642–2.075) alongside the fixed BOU baseline (−10.9 nT observed in 2017).
data_sources_used: none for validation of WIN-068 itself
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 2026 forward projection carries a Kp<2 quiet-day precondition (per WIN-010 detail_evidence) despite the 2017 BOU anchor being Kp≈3 — but this is a WIN-010-side issue, not a WIN-068 issue. WIN-068 itself has no precondition logic because it has no monitor check.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Finding field asserts 'delta_Z = FSF × baseline' but the raw-text formula is 'delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF' (three factors, coverage omitted). See hole #1 below.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py in raw-text/ snapshot. Confirmed by listing raw-text/ contents and by grepping for 068, eclipse_ensemble, and Kim in the predictions files. The dome itself describes WIN-068 as a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble, not a live-validation check.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933 → modernized in Yamazaki & Maute 2021) Sq current system predicts the exact ensemble signature — Y-component increase, X/Z/F decreases, 30–50° magnetic-latitude peak, ~180-min window. The dome relabels the mechanism as 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' in a 'pressurized toroidal cavity' but produces no derivation of the ensemble signature from dome parameters. Its Steelman Evidence Ladder admits this evidence is consistent with both models.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) published the 39-eclipse ensemble analysis in Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054. The dome incorporated it into the WIN list only in V51.0 (early 2026) — an ~8-year gap between data availability and dome 'prediction.' No pre-registration in a timestamping venue independent of the author for WIN-068.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: WIN-068 is a retrospective citation of the 1991–2016 Kim & Chang dataset. The detail_evidence itself states 'The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here does not invoke FSF at all — it is a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) dataset with no dome-derived prediction attached.' FSF = H(r)/r is a legitimate derivation in the 2026 forward projection (WIN-010 scope), but WIN-068 is not that WIN. The dome has no dome-parameter derivation for Y-up/X-Z-F-down, the 30–50° peak, or the 180-min timescale.

holes_found.description: Finding field formula is inaccurate. Current text reads 'The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = FSF × baseline)' — a two-factor form. The raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21 formula is 'delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF' (three factors). The coverage term is a non-trivial factor in the per-station predictions (ranges 0.40–0.95 across the nine 2026 stations) and is the factor both the dome formula and any standard-physics Besselian-elements prediction share in common — so omitting it from the summary-table formula obscures the real fingerprint of the dome-vs-standard discriminator (geographic-latitude FSF vs magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat), after factoring out the shared coverage term — see WIN-010 post-EXP-228 framing).
holes_found.recommendation: In the finding field, replace '(delta_Z = FSF × baseline)' with '(delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF)' to match the raw-text formula and align with the post-EXP-228 WIN-010 discriminator framing. Alternatively, drop the formula entirely from the finding since the finding is supposed to be a summary-table one-liner and the formula is already given verbatim in both detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text.

holes_found.description: Finding field applies a 2026-forward-projection critique to a WIN that the detail_evidence explicitly scopes to the 1991–2016 retrospective ensemble. The finding says 'the absolute magnitude borrows the 2017 BOU eclipse observation (−10.9 nT) as its fitted baseline' — but this is the 2026 forward projection, not WIN-068 content. WIN-068 own claim is the Kim & Chang 39-eclipse ensemble, which does not invoke the 2017 BOU baseline at all (those stations span 1991–2016 and are analyzed by the ensemble own statistical method, not scaled from Boulder). The detail_evidence paragraph 3 makes this clean distinction ('The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here does not invoke FSF at all'), but the summary-table finding conflates the two. A summary-table reader who never clicks through would walk away with an inaccurate picture of which critique applies to which WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the 'absolute magnitude borrows the 2017 BOU observation' clause from the finding (since it applies to the 2026 forward projection, not WIN-068 own ensemble), and replace with the actual WIN-068 critique: 'No dome-derived prediction for the ensemble signature is attached — the 1991–2016 ensemble is a retrospective Kim & Chang citation.' OR (b) explicitly tag the finding as cluster critique by leading with 'Within the WIN-010/025/068 cluster...' so readers understand the scope of the claim.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence paragraph 3 has structural inversion. The paragraph leads with 6+ sentences on standard-physics Sq modeling and then the FSF = H(r)/r concession ('The dome FSF scaling factor is a legitimate geometric derivation — direct recomputation reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999'), and only the LAST clause of the paragraph says 'The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here does not invoke FSF at all — it is a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) dataset with no dome-derived prediction attached.' A reader following the argument in order could walk 80% of the way through the paragraph thinking the FSF concession is the central dome strength on WIN-068, only to learn at the end that FSF is irrelevant to this particular WIN. This weakens the point. WIN-010 legitimately discusses FSF (WIN-010 2026 forward projection uses it); WIN-068 does not. The concession-then-pivot c9-moderate fix addressed the 'by contrast' problem but preserved the inverted ordering.
holes_found.recommendation: Restructure paragraph 3 so the scope is declared first: 'WIN-068 ensemble claim does not invoke FSF at all — it is a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) 1991–2016 dataset. For completeness, we acknowledge the dome FSF scaling factor (relevant to WIN-010 2026 forward projection) is a legitimate geometric derivation reproducing nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999; we retract our prior empirical-fitting framing. Because FSF is not a WIN-068 ingredient, that concession does not save this WIN.' Then the Sq-parameter-sources critique can follow as the positive argument that Chapman/Kim & Chang predict the ensemble signature without any dome parameters.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence paragraph 2 still uses the pre-rename phrasing 'the refraction fix leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate' with cross-reference to Section 1.8 (which EXP-222/HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001 renamed to 'The Coordinate Collapse' and reframed around n(r) contamination of disc-radius coordinates rather than 'refraction cascade / seasonal sun indeterminacy'). The paragraph is self-inoculated ('applicable to the dome own mechanism, not the Std Model Explains verdict above') so it cannot confuse the verdict. However, its cluster vocabulary remains out-of-sync with the rest of the cluster (WIN-010, WIN-025 now use 'Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse)' + 'n(r) 300–6,000× more refractive' language). c9 flagged this as minor and noted EXP-222 explicitly scoped it out; the patch did not touch it.
holes_found.recommendation: Either bring this paragraph into cluster vocabulary (use 'The Coordinate Collapse' title, n(r)-contamination framing) or remove the paragraph — it is the only pre-V51.1 island in the otherwise post-restructure text. If deliberately preserved as a cavity-mechanism-only side-note, retitle the paragraph opener to make the scope crystal clear ('A separate n(r)-contamination concern, distinct from our Chapman/Kim & Chang critique above:').

holes_found.description: Chapman (1933) citation format ambiguous. detail_evidence paragraph 3 reads 'Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3)'; detail_verdict_text paragraph 1 reads 'Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3)'. The '38:3' form is ambiguous — could be Vol 38 Issue 3 or Vol 38 page 3. The actual Chapman paper in Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. Vol 38 (1933) that is commonly cited in Sq/ionospheric-eclipse contexts is the lunar tide paper (38:1–23). Chapman foundational work on the Sq current system is better attributed to the 1919 'Solar diurnal variation of magnetic declination' (Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118) or the Chapman & Bartels (1940) Geomagnetism monograph. Still flagged in c9; unchanged.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) tighten the Chapman citation to a specific paper title + DOI (preferred), or (b) resolve the '38:3' ambiguity by adopting the standard form '38(1), 1–23' if the lunar-tide paper is intended, or (c) substitute the Chapman & Bartels (1940) Geomagnetism monograph as the Sq source (the most accurate anchor for 'the Chapman Sq framework').

holes_found.description: 'The dome Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models' appears in both detail_evidence paragraph 4 and detail_verdict_text paragraph 4 — verbatim duplication. Not wrong but reduces signal density.
holes_found.recommendation: Keep the Steelman reference in detail_verdict_text (where it does real work as a standalone admission against the WIN) and drop the detail_evidence repetition, or vice versa. Currently the same sentence is doing redundant work in both places.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest single-sentence attack on WIN-068 is that the dome own Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse-magnetic evidence at Tier 1 — i.e., the dome itself concedes this evidence is consistent with both models. Leading the finding or the detail_verdict_text with that admission ('The dome itself labels this evidence Tier 1 — consistent with both models — so by the dome own ladder WIN-068 alone cannot be a discriminator') would be rhetorically stronger than the current lead on Chapman/Kim & Chang mechanism, which a reader has to evaluate for themselves. The Tier 1 admission is an own-goal we can simply quote.
sources: raw-text from dome site Steelman Evidence Ladder (cited in detail_evidence paragraph 4 and detail_verdict_text paragraph 4)

stronger_arguments.description: An additional internal-consistency hit the review does not pursue: the dome claims WIN-068 demonstrates 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' via the toroidal cavity. Ionospheric Sq current geometry is set by magnetic-latitude (tilted dipole) geometry — the 30–50° Sq-vortex peak in Kim & Chang data lines up with Earth magnetic latitudes, not geographic latitudes. A dome without Earth dipole field, with a firmament-plate dielectric boundary, would have no first-principles reason to produce a magnetic-latitude-centered signal. This is a discriminator WIN-068 does not currently articulate. The WIN-010 post-EXP-228 reframe (FSF on geographic-lat vs f(mag_lat) on magnetic-lat as the 2026 discriminator) is effectively the sharpened version of this attack, and WIN-068 detail_verdict_text could point at that 2026 test rather than stopping at 'consistent with both models.'
sources: Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble 30–50° mag-lat peak; WIN-010 c7+ FSF-vs-mag_lat discriminator framing

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome FSF = H(r)/r station-scaling derivation is genuine. Solving the dome own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station latitude and normalizing to Boulder reproduces all nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst, with Pearson r = 0.9999. The post-EXP-220/222 retraction of our 'empirical fitting' framing is correct — the dome did derive this piece of its 2026 forward projection from its own geometry.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: FSF is not a WIN-068 ingredient. WIN-068 is the 1991–2016 retrospective Kim & Chang ensemble; the ensemble statistical method does not invoke FSF at all. Even where FSF does apply (WIN-010 2026 forward projection), the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor is imported from the 2017 observation rather than derived, and FSF alone is not uniquely dome-geometric (any smooth monotonic H(r) with the r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form). The 2026 discriminator is the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude residual after factoring out coverage — a clean test WIN-010 now stages, but WIN-068 cannot stage because it is retrospective.

deeper_analysis:
The queue_id 232 patches (03aded8) addressed the c9 critical hole (finding field retracting the empirical-fitting claim) and the c9 moderate hole (detail_evidence 'by contrast' transition). Both were real fixes and they do land — the finding no longer asserts FSF is 'empirically calibrated' and the detail_evidence no longer uses 'by contrast' to introduce the concession. The new wording is quantitatively accurate and the retraction is clean.

The residual weaknesses (holes #1–#3 above) concentrate in the summary-table finding field, which absorbed the concession-then-pivot awkwardly. The finding field is supposed to be a one-line readable claim; it now carries the full concession-then-pivot on a WIN where FSF is not even relevant. Every reader who only reads the summary table (majority of readers) gets the WIN-010 argument dropped into the WIN-068 slot. The detail_evidence paragraph 3 has the same inversion but softer — a reader who clicks through and reads in order still hits the 'does not invoke FSF at all' punchline at the end.

TLDRs read cleanly and the verdict holds. The cross-WIN consistency is finally in sync: WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068 all concede FSF = H(r)/r and all pivot to the absolute-magnitude/2017-BOU/observation-anchored framing. The cluster narrative works. What it does not do is tailor each WIN critique to what that WIN actually invokes — the FSF concession is universal but FSF only applies in WIN-010. Minor editing of the finding and paragraph 3 would fix this without further cluster work.

Advocate-mode check: the strongest dome defender attack on our review is precisely that we made a universal concession (FSF is real) but then deploy it in contexts where FSF does not apply (WIN-068 ensemble). If the concession was honest, the cleanest thing is to let the concession save WIN-010 scaling and show WIN-068 cannot benefit because it has no scaling-equation to save. Current text hedges this.

The TLDR factual-check passed. Both TLDRs are faithful simplifications of the detail text.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018) Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054 — citation re-used from WIN-010/025 cluster; DOI not printed in WIN-068 text but the reference identifies a real paper on 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble analysis.
Yamazaki & Maute (2021) Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences — URL https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fspas.2021.766327/full (not re-verified this cycle; verified in prior cycles).
citations_unchecked: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) — ambiguous '38:3' format (vol:page vs vol:issue); the specific paper title is not given, and Chapman 1933 Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. Vol 38 publications include the lunar-tide paper (38:1–23) and worldwide-disturbance statistics paper. A specific Chapman paper that explicitly derives Sq current eclipse response is better attributed to Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118 or Chapman & Bartels (1940) Geomagnetism. Flagged as a minor hole above.
INTERMAGNET (https://www.intermagnet.org/) — organization URL only, not a specific citation.
